request.question: What do people in the United Kingdom commonly call the location of the room where the entire play "On the Rocks" is set?

title: Pippa Passes, Kentucky
Saluta Rocks
10 Downing Street
Uriconian
On the Rocks (play)
Snag Rocks
Ramp Rocks
Recreation room
Battle of Muhu
Drawing room play
sentences: Pippa Passes is a home rule-class city located along Caney Creek in Knott County, eastern Kentucky, United States.
Its formal name was chosen to honor benefactors of Alice Lloyd College.
After more than a century, its residents commonly call the community as Caney or Caney Creek.
The population was 533 at the time of the year 2010 U.S. Census.
The small city is located in the mountainous Appalachia region, an area of coal mining.
Saluta Rocks ( ) is a group of rocks 1 nautical mile (1.9 km) east of Laurie Point, lying off the south coast and near the west end of South Georgia.
The name Mutt and Jeff was probably given by Lieutenant Commander J.M. Chaplin of the 1926.
The SGS, 1955–56, reported that the name is misleading; there are not two rocks as implied, but a group.
The rocks were renamed by the United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) for the Saluta, a transport of the South Georgia Whaling Co.
for many years.

10 Downing Street, colloquially known in the United Kingdom as Number 10, is the headquarters of the Government of the United Kingdom and the official residence and office of the First Lord of the Treasury, a post which, for much of the 18th and 19th centuries and invariably since 1905, has been held by the Prime Minister.
The Uriconian rocks of Shropshire (Wrekin Terrane) are thought to be potentially related to the Longmyndian Supergroup of the Stretton Hills, Shropshire, United Kingdom.
Current geological profiling of the terranes suggests that the Uriconian rocks are of Precambrian age (Neoproterozoic Phases 2 and 3).
The Uriconian Rocks outcrop to the southeast of the Long Mynd area of the Welsh Borderland Fault System and beyond the Church Stretton Fault which trends northeast-southwest across the area.
The Stretton Hills are composed primarily of arenaceous (quartz rich sand) beds assigned to the Longmyndian Supergroup.
The Longmyndian rocks are a c.6 km thick group of volcaniclastic and bentonitic sediment horizons.

The Uriconian have long thought to be older than the Longmyndian and generally outcrop to the southeast of the latter within northeast-southwest trending lineaments suggesting basement influence for the regional structure.
On the Rocks: A political Comedy (1932) is a play by George Bernard Shaw which deals with the social crisis of the Great Depression.
The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street.
It is noted for its evidence of Shaw's political evolution towards apparent support for dictatorship.
Snag Rocks, ( ) also known as Rocas Bravo, is a cluster of rocks off the west coast of the Antarctic Peninsula.
This cluster of rocks lies mid-channel in the French Passage between the Roca Islands and the Myriad Islands, in the Wilhelm Archipelago.

Photographed from the helicopter of HMS Protector in March 1958, Snag Rocks was so named by United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) because the feature presents a hazard or obstacle to navigation.
Ramp Rocks ( ) are three barren rocks, the largest being 23 m high, lying 2.5 nautical miles (4.6 km) northwest of Johannesen Point, Main Island, at the west extremity of South Georgia.
The name "Laavebrua," a descriptive Norwegian term meaning literally "threshing floor bridge" or "barn bridge," was used for the largest rock by whalers and sealers at South Georgia.
In Norwegian barns used for storing hay, there is a ramp up which the wagons are driven before tipping.
"Laavebrua," which is not strictly translatable, is this ramp.
The United Kingdom Antarctic Place-Names Committee (UK-APC) recommended in 1954 that "Ramp Rock" be approved for the large rock, but in 1976 altered the name to Ramp Rocks to include the three rocks.
The name Låvebrua Island is already approved for an island off Deception Island.
A recreation room (also known as a rec room, rumpus room, or ruckus room) is a room used for a variety of purposes, such as parties, games and other everyday or casual use.
The term is common in the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Canada, but is less common in the United Kingdom where the preferred term is games room.
Often children and teenagers entertain their friends in the rec room, which is often located in the basement, away from the main living areas of the house.
Usually it is a larger space than a living room to have the ability to serve multiple purposes and entertain moderately large groups.
In January 1227, when the sea had frozen hard, a large Crusader army after the call of the Papal legate William of Modena was gathered.
According to the Chronicle of Livonia, 20 000 troops had set out to Muhu from the estuary of Pärnu River and moved along the ice.
Since the ice was said to have been very slippery, they reached the stronghold of Muhu on the ninth day.
After seeing such a large force, the people of Muhu offered truce and allowed themselves to be baptised.
Most of the Germans didn't accept it and so they started besieging the stronghold.
The first rush was fend off with rocks and spears.
The besiegers threw rocks into the stronghold with bricoles, built a turret and undermined the wall.

A drawing room play is a type of play, developed during the Victorian period in the United Kingdom, in which the actions take place in a drawing room or which is designed to be reenacted in the drawing room of a home.

The common practice of entertaining a guest in the home led to the creation of this category of plays and while the drawing room itself has fallen out of favour, the play format has continued to provide a source of entertainment.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What do people in the United Kingdom commonly call the location of the room where the entire play "On the Rocks" is set?
hop1_docs: Eze-Igwe | The Oguta kingdom was established in like manners to the Benin Kingdom of Nigeria.  Many people in Oguta believe that the Oguta people migrated from the ancient Benin Kingdom.  They argue that the administrative set up, titles and common names have similarities to those of Bini people.  Although the people of Oguta do not call their king Oba as the Bini do, both kingdoms have some striking parallels.  The Binis have Iyase, in Oguta it is called Iyasara, there is Ogine among the Bini and Ogana in Oguta.  Palm oil and plantain are called ofigbo and ipa in both Edo language and Oguta dialect to this day.  Although the Oguta people speak a dialect of the Igbo language, some of their words are unmistakably of Bini origin.

Recreation room | A recreation room (also known as a rec room, rumpus room, or ruckus room) is a room used for a variety of purposes, such as parties, games and other everyday or casual use.  The term is common in the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Canada, but is less common in the United Kingdom where the preferred term is games room.  Often children and teenagers entertain their friends in the rec room, which is often located in the basement, away from the main living areas of the house.  Usually it is a larger space than a living room to have the ability to serve multiple purposes and entertain moderately large groups.

Do What You Do (Jermaine Jackson song) | "Do What You Do" is a single by American R&B singer Jermaine Jackson, sibling of singers Michael and Janet Jackson and former member of The Jackson 5.  It was released as the second single his album, which was called "Jermaine Jackson" in the United States and "Dynamite" in the United Kingdom and other countries.
Hey, Hey, What Can I Do | "Hey, Hey, What Can I Do" is a song by the English rock group Led Zeppelin, released in 1970 as the B-side of "Immigrant Song" outside the United Kingdom.

On the Rocks (play) | On the Rocks: A political Comedy (1932) is a play by George Bernard Shaw which deals with the social crisis of the Great Depression.  The entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street.  It is noted for its evidence of Shaw's political evolution towards apparent support for dictatorship.

That's What People Do | That's What People Do is an independently released album by the rock band Thousand Foot Krutch.  Rhyme Animal is the first and only single from this album, released in Canada.  The tracks titled "Rhyme Animal", "Small Town", "Lift It" and "Set It Off" were rerecorded in 2000 for the album Set It Off and "Rhyme Animal" was re-titled "Rhime Animal".  This is the only TFK album to have no music videos, until the 2009 record "Welcome to the Masquerade".  Trevor McNevan is the only member on the album who is still in the band.

Big Tent Revival | Big Tent Revival is a Christian rock band that formed in 1991, toured extensively, disbanded in 2000, and reformed in 2012.  They were featured at the Harvest Crusades.  Their most popular songs were "Two Sets of Jones'", "Choose Life", and "What Would Jesus Do?" .  The first told a story about two different couples in which one trusted in Jesus through the storms of life and the other didn't.  The second was used as an invitational at Harvest Crusade altar calls.  The last was part of the WWJD movement that encouraged people to consider what Jesus would do in real-life situations.
hop1_titles: Do What You Do (Jermaine Jackson song)
Hey, Hey, What Can I Do
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: setting of Shaw's On the Rocks; UK regional term for "recreation room."

- On the Rocks (1932) — play by George Bernard Shaw.  
- The passages state the "entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street."  
- Passage on room terminology: the term "recreation room" is "less common in the United Kingdom" and the "'preferred term is games room'."  
- Missing / ambiguous: the passages do NOT state what people in the UK commonly call 10 Downing Street or the Cabinet Room (bridge entity: "10 Downing Street"); possible colloquial variants (e.g., "No. 10"/"Number Ten") are not given.  
- Next-hop retrievals (priority order):
  1. Fetch "10 Downing Street" (Wikipedia) — inspect lede/infobox and any "Names/Nicknames" or "Popular culture" sections for common UK names (e.g., "No. 10"/"Number Ten").  
  2. Fetch "Cabinet Room" (Wikipedia) — lede/infobox and "Location/Name" details to see any UK naming conventions.  
  3. Fetch "On the Rocks (play)" full article — lede and "Synopsis/Setting" to confirm phrasing.  
  4. If needed, search: "what do people in the United Kingdom call 10 Downing Street" / "No. 10 nickname" / "what is Number Ten called in UK".
response.hop2_query: 10 Downing Street common UK name nickname
hop2_docs: Downing Street memo | The "Downing Street memo" (or the "Downing Street Minutes"), sometimes described by critics of the Iraq War as the "smoking gun memo", is the note of a 23 July 2002 secret meeting of senior British government, defence and intelligence figures discussing the build-up to the war, which included direct reference to classified United States policy of the time.  The name refers to 10 Downing Street, the residence of the British prime minister.

Farthing Common | Farthing Common is an area of common land about 10 km northwest of Folkestone in East Kent.  It is one of the highest points of the North Downs at 185 metres above sea level, and was the junction of the ancient trackway along the Downs from Folkestone, with the Roman road (now the B2068 Stone Street) between Lympne and Canterbury.

List of residents of 10 Downing Street | Number 10 Downing Street is the residence and office of the First Lord of the Treasury as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.  The headquarters of Her Majesty's Government, it is situated on Downing Street in the City of Westminster in London, England.
Down on the Street | "Down on the Street" is a 1984 song by British band Shakatak.  The song is about the joy of nightlife.  It was a big hit, reaching #9 in the UK.

Walking Down Your Street | "Walking Down Your Street" is a song by The Bangles.  It is the fourth single from their album "Different Light".  It was released as a single in 1987 and charted at #11 on the "Billboard" Hot 100, #16 on the UK Singles Chart, and #56 on the Australian Kent Music Report chart.  The next single, "Following", was only released in the UK, making "Walking Down Your Street" the final single from "Different Light" in the US.  The original LP version varies slightly from the 7" single version and the version included with their "Greatest Hits" compilation.

Gainsborough, New South Wales | Gainsborough, New South Wales, Australia is a residential estate forming part of the suburb of Kiama Downs in the Municipality of Kiama.  This is a relatively new urban area bordering Kiama Downs and Jamberoo.  A new section of the Princes Highway was opened to the west of Gainsborough in late 2005.  The name Gainsborough is derived from the original estate name of the development which was based on painters names, you can find many artists names in the suburban streets of Gainsborough.  The Gainsborough Estate was developed by The Homes Corporation of Australia in the early 1980s and the nickname for the suburb was adopted by all of the residents.

Tapio (name) | Tapio is a male given name common in Finland.  The nameday is 18 June.  As of January 2013 there were almost 140,000 people with this name in Finland.  The name originates from the name of the Finnish god of forests, animals, and hunting.  A common nickname for Tapio is Tapsa.  It is listed by the Finnish Population Register Centre as one of the top 10 most popular male given names ever.
hop2_titles: List of residents of 10 Downing Street
response.summary_2: The passages state the "entire play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street." 10 Downing Street is the official residence and office of the British prime minister.
response.answer: The play is set in the Cabinet Room of 10 Downing Street, but the provided summaries do not state what people in the United Kingdom commonly call that location, so a definitive common-name answer cannot be determined from these summaries alone.
